sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A university tested whether brief intervention training would increase helping behavior in emergency simulations. Students were assigned to no training, a short bystander-intervention module, or the same module plus a role-play exercise. During a later staged scenario, researchers recorded the percentage who offered help and the average latency to act. Help rates were 38%, 61%, and 74%, respectively. Mean latency to act was 42, 28, and 19 seconds. Participants in the role-play condition also reported greater confidence in recognizing emergencies.
questions.stem: According to Figure 1, which condition produced the highest helping rate?

explanations.A: Correct. The role-play condition has the tallest helping bar at 74%, exceeding the module-only condition at 61% and the no-training condition at 38%. The figure therefore identifies role-play as the strongest intervention.
explanations.B: The no-training group had the lowest helping rate and cannot be correct.
explanations.C: The module-only condition improved helping, but it still did not reach the role-play condition.
explanations.D: The bars differ widely, so the conditions were not similar.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Helping rate and average latency to intervene across three bystander-training conditions.

questions.stem: The lower latency in the trained groups most directly suggests improvement in what process?

choices.B: Recognizing the situation as requiring action and deciding to intervene
choices.A: Sensory adaptation to the simulation room

explanations.B: Correct. The bystander effect often involves delays at multiple decision points, including noticing the problem, interpreting it as an emergency, and accepting responsibility. Shorter latency after training suggests participants moved through these steps more efficiently.
explanations.A: Sensory adaptation is not the key process being trained or measured here.
explanations.C: Extinction involves weakening a learned response, but the intervention was designed to strengthen helping.
explanations.D: Crystallized intelligence is unrelated to the helping-latency pattern in the passage.

questions.stem: Why might role-play outperform the module alone?

choices.C: Behavioral rehearsal can increase self-efficacy and make the response easier to execute
choices.A: Role-play guarantees identical personalities across students
choices.B: Reading information alone always causes helplessness
choices.D: Role-play removes all ambiguity from every emergency

explanations.C: Correct. Practicing the response can make people feel more capable and can strengthen memory for what to do. That explanation fits the higher confidence reports in the role-play group and helps account for their faster, more frequent intervention.
explanations.A: Training does not make participants identical in personality.
explanations.B: Information alone can still help; the issue is that practice added further benefit.
explanations.D: Role-play may reduce hesitation, but it does not remove all ambiguity in every possible emergency.

questions.stem: If a residence hall wanted to apply these findings, which step would be most consistent with the data?

choices.D: Include active practice scenarios in addition to informational training
choices.A: Provide only a poster listing emergency phone numbers
choices.B: Avoid discussing emergencies so students do not feel anxious
choices.C: Tell students that intervention depends entirely on personality

explanations.D: Correct. The role-play condition showed the strongest performance on both helping frequency and speed. That pattern suggests that active rehearsal should be part of any practical bystander program rather than relying on information alone.
explanations.A: A poster may offer information, but the strongest gains came from practice, not just passive reminders.
explanations.B: Avoiding the topic would remove the training opportunity that improved helping.
explanations.C: The experiment indicates that training mattered, so personality cannot be the entire explanation.

questions.stem: Which additional result would most strengthen a causal interpretation of the role-play advantage?

choices.A: The three groups had similar prior volunteer experience before training
choices.B: Students in the role-play group liked the facilitator more
choices.C: The simulation was held in the same building for all groups
choices.D: Some students described the scenario as realistic

explanations.A: Correct. If prior helping tendencies such as volunteer experience were comparable across groups, then the later differences are more plausibly attributable to the training itself rather than to preexisting prosocial differences.
explanations.B: Liking the facilitator might introduce another explanation instead of strengthening the intended interpretation.
explanations.C: Using the same building standardizes setting but does not directly address baseline helping tendencies.
explanations.D: Perceived realism is useful, but it does not rule out group differences in prior prosocial orientation.

questions.passage: A city youth program examined environmental stressors and aggression during summer recreation sessions. Researchers compared sessions held on mild days, hot crowded days, and hot crowded days after staff implemented additional cooling stations and structured breaks. Mean observed verbal-aggression incidents per session were 3.2, 7.4, and 4.5, respectively. Mean self-reported irritability scores on a 1–10 scale were 4.6, 8.1, and 5.9. Staff noted fewer disputes after the cooling-and-break intervention, even though attendance remained similar.
questions.stem: Which condition showed the highest level of aggression in Figure 2?

choices.B: Hot crowded days without the intervention
choices.C: Hot crowded days with cooling stations and breaks

explanations.B: Correct. The hot crowded condition produced 7.4 aggression incidents per session, the highest value in the figure. Both the mild-day condition and the intervention condition were lower.
explanations.A: Mild days had the fewest incidents, not the most.
explanations.C: The intervention condition reduced incidents relative to the untreated hot crowded condition.
explanations.D: The values differ substantially, so equality is not supported.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Mean aggression incidents and irritability scores across three recreation-session conditions.

questions.stem: The pattern most strongly supports which interpretation?

choices.C: Heat and crowding were associated with higher irritability and more aggression
choices.A: Aggression was unaffected by environmental conditions
choices.B: Cooling stations caused the initial increase in aggression
choices.D: Structured breaks worked only by changing attendance

explanations.C: Correct. Aggression and irritability both rise sharply on hot crowded days, then decline when the setting is modified. This supports the interpretation that environmental stressors contributed to the aggressive pattern.
explanations.A: The figure shows large differences across environmental conditions.
explanations.B: Cooling stations were added later and were associated with a decrease, not the original increase.
explanations.D: Attendance stayed similar, so attendance change is not the main mechanism offered by the passage.

questions.stem: Why might cooling stations and breaks reduce conflict?

choices.D: They likely lowered physiological stress and reduced frustrating interactions
choices.A: They trained participants in classical conditioning
choices.B: They eliminated every source of competition
choices.C: They changed participants’ long-term personality traits

explanations.D: Correct. By reducing discomfort and giving participants structured pauses, the intervention likely reduced arousal and opportunities for tense interaction. That mechanism is consistent with the decline in both irritability and aggression.
explanations.A: Classical conditioning is not the mechanism suggested by the passage.
explanations.B: The intervention could reduce conflict without eliminating all competition or tension.
explanations.C: A brief environmental change would not be expected to alter stable personality traits immediately.

questions.stem: A camp director trying to prevent similar problems should most likely do what?

choices.A: Modify stressful settings by reducing heat load and building in breaks
choices.B: Increase crowding to improve social bonding
choices.C: Remove all supervision so peers resolve disputes independently
choices.D: Ignore discomfort because irritability and aggression are unrelated

explanations.A: Correct. The intervention lowered both irritability and aggression while attendance remained stable. That makes environmental modification a practical prevention strategy for group settings in hot conditions.
explanations.B: The untreated crowded condition showed the opposite pattern.
explanations.C: Less supervision is not supported by the study and could worsen conflict.
explanations.D: The passage shows that irritability and aggression moved together across conditions.

questions.stem: Which design feature most limits causal certainty in this study?

choices.B: Sessions were compared across naturally occurring weather conditions rather than by full random assignment
choices.A: Aggression incidents were counted per session

explanations.B: Correct. Because weather and crowding levels were not fully randomized in the way a laboratory experiment would permit, other session-level differences could contribute to the observed aggression pattern. The data strongly suggest association but leave room for residual confounding.
explanations.A: Counting incidents is an appropriate outcome measure and not the main limitation.
explanations.C: The scale format does not explain the causal limitation.
explanations.D: Similar attendance helps comparability rather than creating the core inferential problem.
